required.title: [Read] At least two of the following dystopian novels:
[Read] At least one work exploring sacred or civilizational narrative:
[Watch and Analyze] At least two films selected for literary quality and worldview content
[Read] At least one work exploring sacred or civilizational narrative:
[Watch and Analyze] At least two films selected for literary quality and worldview content

suggested.title: [Reference] The Hero with a Thousand Faces
suggested.author: Joseph Campbell

supplementary.title: [Watch] Triumph of the Will
supplementary.author: Leni Riefenstahl (selected clips, not the full film)

competence_markers.description: The student can explain how at least one foundational narrative (Exodus, the Gospels, Greek heroic myths) shaped the civilization that tells it
The student can identify the hero's journey pattern in at least two stories from different cultures and discuss its strengths and limitations
The student can distinguish between propaganda and genuine art — identifying the techniques of manipulation and explaining why the distinction matters
The student has read and analyzed at least one dystopian novel, identifying the author's warning and its assumptions about human nature
The student can analyze a film or media narrative using the same critical tools applied to written texts
The student has examined at least one national or community myth, identifying what it includes, omits, and promotes
The student can write a 500-800 word analysis of a narrative's worldview with specific evidence and clear reasoning
The student demonstrates the habit of asking "what does this story want me to believe?" across all media — books, films, news, advertising

narration_prompts.prompt: Choose a foundational narrative (Exodus, the Gospels, Greek heroic myths) and explain how it shaped a civilization.
narration_prompts.detail: What does the story assume about the world? How did those assumptions shape the civilization that believed them?
Give an example of a story that fits and a story that does not. What are the strengths and limits of Campbell's framework?

narration_prompts.prompt: How does propaganda work as story?
narration_prompts.detail: Pick a specific example and analyze it: What story is being told? What does it want you to believe? How does it manipulate your emotions?

narration_prompts.prompt: Compare the warnings of two dystopian novels.
narration_prompts.detail: What danger does each author worry about most? Which warning seems most relevant today? Why?

narration_prompts.prompt: Analyze a film or media narrative.
narration_prompts.detail: What worldview does it encode? How does it use cinematic techniques to support its message?

narration_prompts.prompt: What story does your community or nation tell about itself?
narration_prompts.detail: What does the story include? What does it leave out? What values does it promote?

cross_connections.description: Propaganda analysis is the direct overlap between 3.2.5-B and 1.7.2. The student who can analyze propaganda as narrative can also analyze it as a political weapon. The skills transfer directly.
Sacred narratives studied here as literature are studied in 1.3.1 as expressions of worldview. The student encounters the same material from two angles, deepening understanding of both.

The close reading skills from 3.2.4 provide the analytical tools applied here. The student who can identify what an author is doing at the level of language and structure can also identify what a narrative is doing at the level of worldview.

The national myths and identity stories examined in this packet are products of the historical periods studied in 1.1. History provides the context; 3.2.5 provides the critical framework for evaluating the stories history produces.

Dystopian novels are political philosophy in narrative form. The student who reads *1984* is engaging with questions about the nature of freedom and the mechanisms of power — the same questions studied in 1.4.4.
The capacity to distinguish between honest storytelling and manipulative propaganda requires the character virtues of intellectual honesty and courage developed in IC1.3.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been hearing and reading stories for years. You have learned to ask what stories want you to think. Now you are going to go deeper — learning how stories do not merely teach individual lessons but carry entire worldviews: complete assumptions about human nature, about what is good and bad, about how society should be organized, about what makes life meaningful. And you are going to learn that this power is not limited to ancient myths and fairy tales. It operates in the novels you read, the films you watch, the news stories you encounter, and the advertisements you see every day.

Stories shape civilizations. The Exodus narrative — the story of a people enslaved, liberated, and led to a promised land — shaped Jewish identity for three thousand years and profoundly influenced Western concepts of freedom and justice. The Gospel narratives shaped Western civilization's understanding of sacrifice, mercy, and the value of the individual person. The Greek hero myths shaped Western ideas about glory, fate, and the cost of pride. These are not decorations on top of civilization. They are the operating systems that civilizations run on.

The student who understands this has a form of literacy that most people lack entirely. You will be able to read a novel and see the worldview it encodes. You will be able to watch a film and identify the argument it is making. You will be able to encounter propaganda and recognize that it is not information — it is a story designed to make you believe something without questioning it. This awareness does not make you immune to the power of story. Nothing can. But it makes you conscious of how stories work on you — and consciousness is the beginning of sovereignty.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Sacred narrative as civilizational foundation** — the student understands how foundational stories (Exodus, the Gospels, Greek myths) shaped entire civilizations
2. **The hero's journey as a universal pattern** — the student identifies Joseph Campbell's monomyth across cultures and asks why it recurs
3. **Propaganda analysis** — the student can distinguish between art that explores a genuine question and propaganda that sells a predetermined answer
4. **Dystopian fiction as warning** — the student reads dystopian novels as arguments about human nature and the future
5. **Film and media as narrative** — the student applies literary analysis tools to film, television, and media
6. **National myths and identity stories** — the student examines how communities construct narratives about themselves and what those narratives include and omit
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Sacred Narrative and Civilization

Every civilization is built on stories. Not just any stories — foundational stories that answer the biggest questions a people can ask: Where did we come from? What are we for? What is right and wrong? What happens when we die?

**The Exodus narrative** is one of the most powerful foundational stories in human history. A people enslaved, crying out for deliverance. A leader called by God. Plagues, liberation, a journey through the wilderness, laws given on a mountain, a promised land. This story shaped Jewish identity for three millennia. It provided the framework for the American Revolution ("Let my people go" was applied to British tyranny). It provided the framework for the civil rights movement (Martin Luther King Jr. explicitly cast the struggle for racial justice in Exodus terms). It provided the framework for liberation theology across Latin America. One story, told and retold across centuries, shaping how millions of people understand freedom, justice, and the relationship between God and human suffering.

**The Gospel narratives** — the story of Jesus of Nazareth: his teaching, his healings, his suffering, his death, his resurrection — shaped Western civilization at the deepest level. The idea that God would become human and suffer willingly. The idea that the last shall be first. The idea that mercy is more powerful than vengeance. The idea that every individual person has infinite value. Whether or not you believe these claims are literally true, you cannot understand Western art, law, literature, philosophy, or politics without understanding the narrative that generated them.

**The Greek heroic tradition** — from Homer's *Iliad* and *Odyssey* through the tragedies of Sophocles and Euripides — encoded a different set of assumptions: that glory is the highest aim of life, that fate is inescapable, that pride (hubris) is the deadliest sin, that even heroes are subject to forces beyond their control. Greek narrative shaped Western concepts of heroism, tragedy, and the relationship between human will and cosmic order.

These are not the only foundational narratives. Every civilization has them. But you must understand these three because they are the narratives that built the civilization you live in — and you cannot navigate that civilization intelligently without knowing its operating stories.

### Part 2: The Hero's Journey

Joseph Campbell, a scholar of mythology, noticed that hero stories from around the world follow a remarkably similar pattern. He called it the "monomyth" or "the hero's journey":

1. **The Ordinary World** — The hero lives in a normal, familiar world.
2. **The Call to Adventure** — Something disrupts the ordinary world and calls the hero to action.
3. **Refusal of the Call** — The hero hesitates or resists.
4. **Meeting the Mentor** — The hero encounters a guide who provides wisdom or tools.
5. **Crossing the Threshold** — The hero leaves the ordinary world and enters the unknown.
6. **Tests, Allies, Enemies** — The hero faces challenges and discovers who can be trusted.
7. **The Ordeal** — The hero faces the greatest challenge — often a confrontation with death or the deepest fear.
8. **The Reward** — The hero gains something — knowledge, a treasure, a new ability.
9. **The Return** — The hero brings what they have gained back to the ordinary world.

This pattern appears in Gilgamesh, in the Odyssey, in the story of Moses, in Star Wars, in Harry Potter, in countless folk tales and modern films. The question is: why? Why do humans everywhere tell the same basic story?

Campbell argued that the hero's journey reflects something universal about human experience — the process of growth, of facing fear, of being transformed by difficulty and returning with something valuable. Whether this is true, or whether Campbell's framework oversimplifies the rich diversity of world storytelling, is a question worth debating. The framework is useful, but it has limits — not every great story fits the pattern, and forcing stories into the mold can distort them.

### Part 3: Propaganda — When Story Becomes a Weapon

Stories can be used to illuminate truth. They can also be used to conceal it. Propaganda is the deliberate use of narrative to manipulate what people believe and how they behave — not through evidence and argument, but through emotional manipulation, simplification, and the selective presentation of reality.

**How propaganda works as story:**
- **Simplification:** Complex situations are reduced to simple narratives with clear heroes and villains. "We are good. They are evil."
- **Emotional manipulation:** Images, music, and language are chosen to trigger specific emotional responses — fear, anger, pride, disgust — rather than to inform.
- **Selective storytelling:** Inconvenient facts are omitted. Context is removed. The narrative shows only what supports its message.
- **Repetition:** The story is told again and again until it feels like common sense rather than a particular perspective.
- **Identification:** The audience is invited to see themselves as the hero of the story — the righteous, the oppressed, the chosen.

**Examples to study:**
- Soviet socialist realism: literature and art required to depict the glorious worker building the socialist future. Stories that questioned the state were suppressed.
- Nazi propaganda: Leni Riefenstahl's *Triumph of the Will* used film technique — soaring camera angles, dramatic music, massive crowds — to make the Nazi movement look beautiful, powerful, and inevitable.
- Wartime propaganda posters from any era: examine how the enemy is depicted, how "our" soldiers are depicted, and what emotional response the image is designed to produce.

The critical skill is learning to ask: Is this story trying to help me understand something, or is it trying to make me feel something so I stop thinking? Art that explores a genuine question invites you to think. Propaganda that sells a predetermined answer wants you to stop thinking and start obeying.

### Part 4: Dystopian Fiction as Warning

Dystopian novels are thought experiments: What would happen if a specific danger went unchecked? Each novel is an argument about human nature and the shape of tyranny.

**Orwell's *1984*:** The danger is totalitarian control of information. The state controls language ("Newspeak"), history (rewritten constantly), and even thought ("thoughtcrime"). The mechanism of control is fear, surveillance, and the destruction of private truth. Orwell's warning: when the state controls what is true, freedom is impossible.

**Huxley's *Brave New World*:** The danger is not tyranny through fear but tyranny through pleasure. Citizens are kept docile through entertainment, drugs ("soma"), and engineered comfort. No one rebels because no one is unhappy — and no one is unhappy because no one thinks deeply enough to notice what they have lost. Huxley's warning: the most effective tyranny is the one people do not recognize because they are too comfortable to care.

**Bradbury's *Fahrenheit 451*:** The danger is the voluntary abandonment of reading and thought. Books are burned not because a dictator imposed it, but because people stopped wanting to read. The fire department exists because the public demanded the destruction of anything complex or challenging. Bradbury's warning: the most insidious censorship comes not from the state but from a culture that prefers entertainment to truth.

**Orwell's *Animal Farm*:** The danger is revolution betrayed — the pattern where those who overthrow tyrants become tyrants themselves. "All animals are equal, but some animals are more equal than others." Orwell's warning: power corrupts, and the language of liberation can be used to justify new oppression.

Each of these novels makes specific assumptions about human nature. Each warns about a specific path to tyranny. Which warnings have proved most accurate? That is a question worth discussing.

### Part 5: Film and Media as Narrative

Everything you have learned about stories in books applies equally to films, television shows, advertisements, and news coverage. They all tell stories. They all encode worldviews. They all want you to believe something.

When you analyze a film, ask the same questions you ask of a novel:
- What worldview does this film encode? What does it assume about human nature?
- Who is the hero? What qualities does the film reward?
- Who is the villain? How are they depicted? Is the depiction fair or a simplification?
- What does the film want you to feel? How does it manipulate those feelings — through music, camera angles, editing, dialogue?
- What would someone who disagrees with this film's message say about it?

This is not about ruining entertainment. It is about being conscious of the arguments that entertainment is making — because if you are not conscious of them, they shape your thinking without your permission.

### Part 6: The Stories We Tell About Ourselves

Every family, community, and nation tells stories about itself. "We are a nation of immigrants." "We are a chosen people." "We pulled ourselves up by our bootstraps." "We survived against all odds." These stories are powerful because they define identity — they tell the people within the community who they are and what they value.

These identity stories always include some things and omit others. The American frontier narrative celebrates courage, independence, and self-reliance — and often omits the displacement and destruction of indigenous peoples. The British "Blitz spirit" narrative celebrates resilience and unity during World War II — and often omits class inequalities and the experience of colonial subjects fighting and dying for an empire that did not treat them as equals.

Understanding the stories your own community tells about itself — and asking what those stories include, what they omit, and what values they promote — is one of the most important forms of critical awareness you can develop. It does not require cynicism. You can love your country and still ask honestly: what does our national story leave out?
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Sacred Narrative Analysis [Early in Packet]
Read a significant section of a sacred or foundational narrative (the Exodus story, a Gospel narrative, or a selection from the Odyssey). After reading, the student writes a 300-500 word analysis: What does this narrative assume about human nature? About the relationship between humans and the divine (or fate)? How has this narrative shaped the civilization that tells it? Discuss with the mentor.

### Study 2: Hero's Journey Mapping [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Choose two stories from different eras or cultures (one ancient, one modern — e.g., the Odyssey and Star Wars, or the Exodus and Harry Potter). Map both onto Campbell's hero's journey framework. Where does the pattern fit? Where does it not? Write a comparison of 500 words. Discuss: Does the hero's journey pattern reveal something universal, or does it oversimplify?

### Study 3: Dystopian Novel Study [Core of Packet]
Read at least one dystopian novel completely. Write a 500-800 word analysis addressing: What specific danger is the author warning about? What assumptions about human nature underlie the novel? Which of the novel's warnings seem most relevant today? Why? Discuss with the mentor.

### Study 4: Propaganda Analysis [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Study at least three examples of propaganda from different eras (wartime posters, political films, advertising campaigns). For each, identify: What story is being told? Who is the hero? Who is the villain? What emotional response is being manipulated? What facts are omitted? Write a comparison of 500 words. Discuss: How do you distinguish propaganda from art?

### Study 5: Film Analysis [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Watch a film selected for its worldview content (from the resource list). Write a 500-word analysis: What worldview does the film encode? What does it assume about human nature? How does it use visual and auditory techniques (music, camera, editing) to support its message? Compare the film's storytelling tools to those available in a novel.

### Study 6: National Myth Examination [Late Packet]
Choose a national myth or identity story (the American frontier narrative, the French Revolution narrative, the "land of the free" narrative, or another relevant to the student's own community). Research: What does this story include? What does it omit? What values does it promote? Write a 500-word analysis that is honest but not cynical — the goal is understanding, not debunking.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Sacred Narrative Analysis
**Developing well:** The student can explain how a foundational narrative shaped a civilization — with specific examples of how the narrative's assumptions influenced law, art, politics, or culture. The analysis is respectful but analytical.

**Developing slowly:** The student either dismisses sacred narratives ("just myths") or accepts them uncritically without analysis.

### 2. Propaganda Detection
**Developing well:** The student can identify propaganda techniques in historical and contemporary examples. They can explain the difference between art that explores a question and propaganda that sells an answer. Their analysis includes specific evidence.

**Developing slowly:** The student either cannot identify propaganda or calls everything propaganda without distinguishing between degrees of manipulation.

### 3. Dystopian Analysis
**Developing well:** The student can articulate the specific warning a dystopian novel makes and connect it to real-world patterns. They can identify the novel's assumptions about human nature.

**Developing slowly:** The student reads dystopian novels as adventure stories without engaging with their ideas.

### 4. Media Literacy
**Developing well:** The student applies narrative analysis tools to films, news, and advertising. They ask "what does this want me to believe?" as a habit, not just as an assignment.

**Developing slowly:** The student can analyze assigned examples but does not apply the skill to their daily media consumption.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Making Cynicism the Goal
The student who concludes "all stories are manipulation" has failed the packet. The goal is discernment, not cynicism. Great literature genuinely illuminates human experience. Sacred narratives genuinely sustain communities. The distinction between honest storytelling and manipulative narrative is real — and the student must learn to make it.

### Avoiding Uncomfortable Analysis
If the student's own community or religion has a foundational narrative, analyzing it critically may be uncomfortable. Do not avoid this. The student who can analyze every community's stories except their own has not developed the skill. Approach with sensitivity but do not skip it.

### Reducing Films to Themes
Film analysis should not be "this film is about friendship." It should engage with how the film works — camera angles, music, editing, dialogue — and how those techniques serve the narrative's worldview. If the student is only identifying themes without analyzing technique, push deeper.
